conversation.message: I have been smoking cigarettes for 15 years, about a pack a day.
You've been smoking a pack a day for 15 years, that's a significant habit. What time of day do you usually have your first cigarette?
Mostly after meals and when I am stressed at work.

It sounds like those two situations, after meals and when stressed at work, have become really tied to your smoking habit. What do you think would happen to your relationships with your family if you were to quit smoking?
My doctor told me my lungs are getting worse. My kids also keep asking me to stop.

It sounds like your doctor's concerns and your kids' requests are really resonating with you, making you want to quit for their sake. Are you willing to try a technique like deep breathing exercises to calm your body when you feel the urge to smoke after meals or during work stress?
I tried nicotine patches once but I kept forgetting to put them on.
You found that nicotine patches didn't help you quit last time, could you tell me why you think you forgot to use them?
I want to quit completely. I just do not know where to start.

You've mentioned wanting to quit completely, which shows a strong motivation to change. Can you think of any times in the past when you were able to cut down on smoking or go a certain amount of time without smoking, even if it was just for a short period?
Maybe I can try keeping gum with me for after meals. That sounds doable.

You're considering keeping gum with you for after meals, which is a great first step. What do you think about trying to chew the gum slowly, so the nicotine can be absorbed more gradually through your mouth, rather than just popping it in your mouth quickly?
I smoke bidis, about 20 a day for 30 years.
You've been smoking bidis for 30 years, that's a long time. What situations or times of the day make you most likely to reach for a bidi?
I do not know if I really want to quit. My son keeps pushing me.

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about quitting, and your son's pressure is making you feel a bit defensive. Are you worried that your son's expectations are more important to you than your own desire to quit?
I smoke most in the morning after tea and when I feel bored at home.

You're starting to notice some patterns in your smoking, like after tea in the morning. Have you ever tried delaying your morning bidi by just 10 minutes after you finish your tea to see if the craving passes?
I never tried anything before. I always thought I could stop on my own.

It sounds like you've always relied on your own willpower to quit, but that hasn't worked for you in the past. Can you think of anything else that might have helped you manage those morning cravings after tea, even if it was just for a short time?
My breathing is getting bad lately. Maybe that is a reason to try.

It sounds like you're feeling motivated to quit for your own health reasons, especially since your breathing is getting worse. Would you be open to trying some simple deep breathing exercises to see if they can help calm your body and reduce your craving for a bidi in those morning moments?
Okay I will think about it. Maybe small steps.

You're considering taking small steps towards quitting, which is a great place to start. What do you think about trying a simple distraction technique, like taking a short walk outside after your morning tea, to see if it can help shift your focus away from the craving for a bidi?
I smoke about 5 cigarettes a day, been doing it for 2 years.

It sounds like you've been smoking for a relatively short period, but you're still looking to quit - that takes a lot of courage. What situations or times of the day make you most likely to reach for a cigarette?
Mainly when I am with friends who smoke or during exam stress.

It sounds like these situations can be really tough for you, and it's great that you're aware of them. How do you think quitting will impact your relationships with your friends, and are you worried about how they'll react?
I want to reduce first. I am not ready to quit completely yet.

You're feeling a bit hesitant about quitting completely, but you're willing to take the first step by reducing your smoking. That shows a lot of courage, and it's a great place to start. How do you think you could use your daily routine to help you cut down on the number of cigarettes you smoke each day?
I tried avoiding parties for a week but ended up smoking more during exams.

It sounds like avoiding social situations didn't quite work out for you, and exams remain a tough time. Can you think of anything else besides avoiding parties that could help you stay on track during exam periods?
I think gum or something to keep my hands busy might help.

You're considering gum to keep your hands busy, which is a great idea. Would you be willing to try drawing or doodling as an alternative to gum, just to see if it might be a helpful distraction for you?
I have been smoking two packs a day for 20 years.
That's a long time with cigarettes, and it's clear you're deeply entrenched in the habit. What do you usually do after waking up in the morning - is smoking one of the first things you do?
I smoke after every meal, with my morning coffee, and when I feel anxious.
It sounds like those routines are very ingrained for you, and it's no wonder you associate them with smoking. Have you ever tried quitting in the past, and if so, what happened?
I want to quit because I had chest pain last month and my doctor is worried.

Your concern for your health is a strong motivation to quit, and it's great that your doctor is supporting you in this decision. 

What do you think about setting a quit date and having a plan in place to avoid those situations that trigger your smoking, like after meals or when you're feeling anxious?
I tried nicotine gum, patches, and cold turkey. None of them worked for long.

You've tried those methods before, and they didn't stick. Have you ever considered trying to replace the act of smoking with a different activity, like going for a short walk, when you feel the urge to smoke?
I always go back when I get very stressed. That is my biggest problem.

It sounds like stress is a major challenge for you when it comes to quitting. Would you be open to trying a short walk or some light physical activity when you feel stressed, like you mentioned, to see if it helps distract you from the urge to smoke?
I think I need something for the stress specifically, not just the craving.

It sounds like you're looking for a way to address the stress that often leads to smoking. Would you be open to trying a change of environment, like taking a few minutes to step outside for some fresh air, when you feel that stress building up?
I smoke. Have been for about 10 years.

It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge your smoking habit, and I'm glad you're here. Can you tell me more about what triggers you to smoke, like when you first reach for a cigarette in a typical day?
I do not know. Sometimes after food I guess.

It sounds like after meals might be a challenging time for you, and that's a good starting point for us to work with. Can you think of a specific meal or situation where you've noticed yourself reaching for a cigarette right after eating?
My wife wants me to stop.

It sounds like your wife's desire for you to quit is a big motivator for you. Would you be open to trying something new after meals, like getting up from the table to do something else, to help break the habit of reaching for a cigarette?
I have not really tried anything.

It takes a big step to admit that you haven't tried anything new yet, and I'm here to help you explore some options. Would you be open to trying a simple technique called the 4D technique, which could help you manage cravings, especially after meals?
I am not sure I am ready.
You're feeling uncertain about trying something new right now. Can you tell me what's holding you back from giving it a shot?

I have been smoking bidis for 25 years, about 15 a day. I want to quit because of my health and my grandchildren. I tried nicotine gum before but did not like the taste. I usually smoke after meals and when I am stressed.

You've been smoking bidis for a long time, and it's great that you're motivated to quit for your health and your grandchildren. Given that you didn't like the taste of nicotine gum, would you be open to trying a different nicotine replacement therapy, like a patch, that might be less noticeable but still help manage cravings?
Yes I am serious about quitting this time.
I can see the determination in your voice, and I'm so proud of you for taking this step towards a healthier future for yourself and your loved ones.
The stress is the hardest part. Work and money problems.

It sounds like stress is overwhelming you, and it's connected to your work and financial worries. Can you tell me more about what happens in your body when you feel stressed, like a physical sensation or a thought pattern?
I have not really tried anything for stress specifically.

So you haven't tried any specific techniques to manage your stress, which is understandable given everything you're going through. Would you be open to trying a short walk or some light physical activity when you feel stressed, like taking a few turns around the block, to see if that helps distract you from cravings and calm your mind?
Maybe I can try something for after meals first. That seems easier.

Let's see if we can make after-meals a manageable time for you. What if, right after you finish eating, you get up from the table and take a short walk around the block before reaching for anything else?
I smoke cigarettes, about 10 a day for 8 years.
You've been smoking for 8 years, that's a long time, and about 10 cigarettes a day is a significant habit. What do you usually do right after waking up in the morning?
Stress and boredom mostly. Also after eating.

You're sharing some of your common triggers with me - stress, boredom, and after eating. Can you tell me if you feel like stress is a stronger trigger for you, or if it's more of a combination of all three?
I want to quit for my health mainly.

You're clear about why you want to quit, it's for your health. Have you ever thought about getting rid of all your cigarettes, lighters, and ashtrays for a while, so those reminders aren't there when you're trying to quit?
What do you think I should do? Just tell me what works.

I think we've gotten to a point where we need to focus on practical steps, and one thing that might be helpful is to start changing your environment when you feel a craving coming on - try moving to another room, going outside for some fresh air, or starting a different activity.
Okay I will try that. What if it does not work though?
It takes a lot of courage to try new approaches, and I'll be here to support you however you need it.
I use chewing tobacco, about 5 pouches a day for 12 years.
It takes a lot of courage to share that, and I appreciate your honesty. What's been going through your mind lately that makes you reach for that 5th or 6th pouch of the day?
After meals and during work mainly. It helps me concentrate.

It sounds like those moments after meals and during work are really challenging for you, and the chewing tobacco seems to help you focus. Can you tell me more about what would happen if you didn't have that help?
I want to stop because my dentist said there is some damage to my gums.
You're really motivated to quit for your own health, and that's amazing. Have you ever tried chewing nicotine gum to help with cravings, or is that something you'd be open to trying?
I tried stopping once but got very irritable and could not focus at work.

You said quitting once was really tough for you, especially at work when you need to focus. Now that we understand what didn't work last time, can you think of any moments during that quit attempt when you felt like you were doing okay, even if it was just a little bit?
Maybe I can reduce first and see how it goes.

You're thinking of taking a gradual approach by reducing your chewing, which is a great idea. What do you think about taking a short walk after meals or during work breaks, just to get moving and shift your focus a bit?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. Been smoking for 15 years.
That's a lot of cigarettes a day, I can sense how difficult it must be for you. When do you usually have a cigarette, is it throughout the day or mostly at certain times?
I smoke when I feel like it. There is no specific trigger.
It sounds like you have a pretty flexible smoking routine, but that can also make it harder to quit. Do you ever find yourself reaching for a cigarette when you're feeling anxious or overwhelmed?
I am here because my doctor sent me. I do not particularly want to quit.

It sounds like you're not feeling super motivated to quit right now, which is completely understandable. What do you think might happen if you were to quit smoking, do you think it would make a big difference in your life?
I have tried before and it does not work. Quitting just makes me miserable.

You're feeling pretty discouraged about quitting, and past attempts haven't worked out for you. What did you think about your previous attempts, was there anything that made you think quitting was worth trying again, even if it didn't stick?
Fine. What is the point of all these questions anyway.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit frustrated with our conversation right now, like it's not really getting anywhere. Is frustration a feeling you experience often when you're thinking about quitting smoking?
I have been smoking for 7 years, about 12 cigarettes a day.

You've been smoking for quite a while now, and it's clear it's become a significant part of your daily routine. What time of day do you usually start smoking, and is there anything in particular that prompts you to light up?
I smoke when I am anxious and in social situations with other smokers.

It sounds like anxiety and being around other smokers are making it really hard for you to quit. Can you think of any ways you might be able to reduce your exposure to situations where you feel anxious and around other smokers?
I want to quit because I am planning to have a baby soon.

That's a wonderful reason to quit, wanting to be a healthy parent for your future baby. How do you think your motivation to quit will be affected by the mixed feelings you mentioned earlier, like feeling unsure and anxious about quitting?
I tried cold turkey twice. The second time I lasted 3 months then relapsed at a party.

Cold turkey didn't work out for you last time, which is totally understandable. Can you tell me what was going through your mind when you relapsed at the party - was it the immediate urge to smoke, or something else?
Social situations are the hardest. I do not know how to say no.

It sounds like you're really struggling with turning down cigarettes in social situations. Have you ever thought about having a friend or family member who knows you're trying to quit and who could help support you through these tough moments?
Okay I think I can try having a response ready when someone offers me a cigarette.

Having a response ready is a great start. What do you think about practicing saying "no" to a cigarette by saying it out loud a few times in front of a mirror, before you're actually in a situation where someone offers you one?
Yes that sounds like a good plan. I will try it this weekend.
It sounds like you're feeling more confident and prepared to take on this challenge – wish you all the best in your progress.
